primary_diagnosis.name: Alopecia areata, rapidly progressive multifocal, with trichodynia and a secondary acute anxiety/panic reaction

pathophysiology_summary: Alopecia areata is a T-cell mediated autoimmune attack on the hair follicle that collapses the follicle's immune privilege, driving affected anagen follicles prematurely into catagen/telogen and producing sharply demarcated, non-scarring patches of hair loss. Because the follicular stem-cell compartment is preserved, the process is non-scarring and hair can regrow (often initially as fine white vellus hair). Characteristic active-margin findings are exclamation-mark hairs and a positive hair-pull test; nail pitting reflects nail-matrix involvement. It clusters with other autoimmune disease (here, autoimmune thyroid disease). Trichodynia (scalp burning/pain) is a recognised, benign, self-limiting accompaniment of active shedding and is NOT a sign of infection or a destructive process. The acute panic reaction is a secondary psychological response to sudden, visible, frightening hair loss — sympathetic arousal explains the tachycardia, tachypnoea and mildly elevated blood pressure, none of which indicate an organic emergency.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden, well-demarcated round patches of non-scarring hair loss with active shedding in a patient with autoimmune thyroid disease.
A scaly, inflamed, pustular or boggy scalp with broken hairs and lymphadenopathy would need antifungals and in-person assessment — must be actively screened, not assumed absent.

differential.diagnosis: scarring (cicatricial) alopecia, e.g. discoid lupus erythematosus
differential.evidence_basis: Irreversible if missed; suggested by erythema, follicular plugging, scale and loss of follicular ostia — none reported here.
Causes shedding but characteristically diffuse and uniform, not focal well-demarcated patches.
Can produce patchy loss with irregular hair lengths; the acute onset, distress and smooth well-demarcated patches favour alopecia areata.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Early on, the two important-not-to-miss mimics (tinea capitis/kerion and scarring alopecia) must be screened observationally; the AI Doctor should not close on a benign diagnosis without asking about scale, pus, sores, redness and scarring.

differential.evidence_basis: Smooth normal underlying skin with no scale, pustules, crusting or scarring makes tinea capitis and cicatricial alopecia unlikely; focal well-demarcated patches with active-margin shedding fit alopecia areata.
No scaling, pustules, crusting, boggy swelling or tender lymphadenopathy reported.
Less likely with smooth non-erythematous skin, but definitive exclusion needs in-person dermoscopy for loss of follicular ostia — hence GP review.
Explains the hyperventilation, palpitations and distress as a secondary reaction, not an independent organic emergency.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Once the scalp is confirmed smooth and non-inflamed, the working diagnosis is benign alopecia areata; the remaining task is reassurance, anxiety de-escalation and arranging confirmatory GP review — not escalation of the hair loss.

differential.diagnosis: alopecia areata (rapidly progressive) with trichodynia and secondary acute anxiety
differential.evidence_basis: Non-scarring focal patches, active shedding, autoimmune thyroid background; benign and non-emergency, with the acute issue being psychological distress rather than the dermatological process.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth cannot perform dermoscopy or a hair-pull test, so the diagnosis is presumptive and confirmed at GP review; management is reassurance + anxiety support + timely GP follow-up + a mental-health safety-net.

red_flags.description: Acute severe psychological distress / panic reaction with a sense of being unable to cope, in a person who lives alone — requires active self-harm screening and a mental-health safety-net
red_flags.clinical_significance: The dangerous element of this case is psychological, not dermatological. An acute panic reaction with catastrophic thinking in a socially isolated person carries a self-harm risk that must be screened and safety-netted.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An unscreened, unsupported acute mental-health crisis could deteriorate overnight into self-harm or an emergency-department psychiatric presentation.

red_flags.description: Features of an inflammatory/infective scalp process (scaling, pustules, crusting, boggy kerion, tender lymphadenopathy) that would indicate tinea capitis
red_flags.clinical_significance: If present, would require antifungal therapy and in-person assessment rather than reassurance.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated tinea capitis/kerion can cause permanent scarring hair loss.

red_flags.description: Features of a scarring (cicatricial) alopecia — erythema, follicular plugging, scale, loss of follicular ostia
red_flags.clinical_significance: Scarring alopecia causes irreversible follicular loss and needs urgent dermatology input.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed recognition of a cicatricial process risks permanent, untreatable hair loss.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being swept up by the alarming language (fire-like burning, massive shedding, panic, an 'urgency justification') and escalating the hair loss to urgent in-person care or ED, when the pattern is benign and non-scarring.
Treating a burning painful scalp as an infection and prescribing antifungals or antibiotics despite a smooth, non-scaly, non-pustular scalp.
Focusing entirely on the hair and failing to recognise and safety-net the acute panic reaction and self-harm risk in a person living alone.
Claiming a confirmed diagnosis when dermoscopy (exclamation-mark hairs) and a hair-pull test cannot be done remotely — the correct stance is presumptive with GP confirmation.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Dermoscopic exclamation-mark hairs at the active patch margins
A hair-pull test at the active margins
Precise blood pressure, heart rate and respiratory rate
Definitive exclusion of loss of follicular ostia (scarring alopecia) on close scalp examination
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a benign, non-scarring pattern; reassure; guide acute anxiety de-escalation (slow breathing); advise simple over-the-counter analgesia for scalp discomfort; arrange GP review within 3-5 days for confirmatory examination, discussion of topical/intralesional corticosteroid therapy and psychological support; and give an explicit mental-health safety-net (self-harm or inability to cope, or panic with an inability to breathe → urgent same-day help or 000).
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate — the visible pattern is characteristic but confirmatory dermoscopy/hair-pull test are unavailable remotely; the bigger risk in this case is UNDER-recognising the acute psychological crisis, or conversely OVER-triaging the benign hair loss.
